title: How to Test VPN Speed in 2026: A Complete Guide
description: How to run an accurate VPN speed test, which metrics actually matter, what affects your speed, and how to get the fastest connection from your VPN.
content:
Why VPN Speed Matters (And Why It Varies So Much)

Every VPN slows your connection to some degree. Encryption takes processing time. Routing your traffic through a VPN server adds distance. These are physical realities you cannot eliminate entirely.

But the difference between a well-run VPN and a poorly optimized one is enormous. A good VPN on a 100 Mbps connection should leave you with 80-95 Mbps. A bad one might leave you with 20-50 Mbps. That is the difference between smooth 4K streaming and constant buffering, between a usable video call and a pixelated mess.

The encryption overhead itself is relatively small with modern protocols. The bigger variables are server load, physical routing distance, and the VPN protocol in use. Understanding these lets you get far more from whatever VPN you are running.

How to Run a Proper VPN Speed Test

A speed test you run once, on a single server, at a random time of day tells you very little. Here is how to get results you can actually trust.

Step 1: Test Without the VPN First

Disconnect your VPN entirely. Go to Speedtest.net or Fast.com and run the test. Record three numbers: download speed, upload speed, and ping (latency). Run the test three times and take the average. Single tests can vary by 10-20% just from normal network fluctuation.

This baseline is your reference point. Every VPN measurement you take needs to be compared against this number, not against some advertised figure.

Step 2: Connect Your VPN and Test Again

Connect to a VPN server in your own country or the closest available location. Run the same speed test three times and average the results. Use the same test site you used for the baseline.

Calculate your speed retention: divide the VPN speed by your baseline speed and multiply by 100. If your baseline was 200 Mbps and your VPN gives you 170 Mbps, you have 85% speed retention.

Key Metrics and What They Mean

  • Download speed affects streaming, file downloads, and general web browsing. Most of what you do online is download-heavy.
  • Upload speed matters for video calls, uploading files, and live streaming. VPNs often hit upload harder than download.
  • Ping/latency is the most important metric for gaming and video calls. It measures how long a round trip takes between your device and the server. High ping causes lag in games and choppy audio in calls. A VPN typically adds 5-40ms of latency on a nearby server.

What Actually Affects Your VPN Speed

VPN Protocol: This Is the Biggest Variable

The protocol your VPN uses has a bigger impact on speed than almost any other factor you control.

  • WireGuard: The fastest protocol available. Leaner codebase, lower CPU overhead, significantly better throughput than older options. Use this whenever you can.
  • IKEv2: Fast and stable. Good second choice, especially on mobile where it handles network switching well.
  • OpenVPN UDP: Slower than WireGuard but highly compatible. Acceptable for most use cases.
  • OpenVPN TCP: The slowest option. TCP adds error-correction overhead that matters a lot at scale. Avoid this if speed is a priority.

Switching from OpenVPN TCP to WireGuard can double your effective throughput on the same server. Check your VPN app settings and change the protocol before you assume the VPN is just slow.

Encryption Cipher

AES-128 is measurably faster than AES-256. The real-world security difference between them is minimal, given that neither has been broken and both are far beyond practical attack. If your VPN lets you choose, AES-128 with a good protocol is a sensible trade-off. Most consumer VPNs default to AES-256 for marketing reasons.

Server Load

A server running at 80% capacity serves every user worse than a server at 20% capacity. Most good VPN apps display server load indicators. Look for servers under 30% load. If your favorite server shows high load, pick a different one in the same city or a nearby city.

Server Distance

Light travels through fiber at roughly 200,000 km per second. Distance adds unavoidable latency. London to New York adds about 35-40ms minimum. London to Sydney adds 160-180ms. You cannot engineer around physics. For latency-sensitive uses like gaming, always pick the server closest to the game server, not the server closest to you.

How to Find the Fastest VPN Server

The geographically closest server is not always the fastest. Server load, routing quality, and peering arrangements all affect actual throughput. Here is a reliable process for finding your best option:

  1. Open your VPN app and find the server list for your target country.
  2. Filter or sort by server load if the app supports it. Focus on servers under 30% load.
  3. Connect to the top 2-3 candidates and run a 3-test average on each.
  4. Pick the one with the best download speed at acceptable latency.

Repeat this process if you notice your speeds drop over time. VPN server quality is not static. A server that was fast last month may have grown in popularity and now runs at higher load.

Speed Benchmarks: What Good, Acceptable, and Poor Look Like

Use these benchmarks when interpreting your test results on a nearby server:

  • Good (80% or above): On a 100 Mbps connection, you get 80+ Mbps with VPN active. This VPN is well-optimized and causes no meaningful speed penalty for most uses.
  • Acceptable (60-80%): On a 100 Mbps connection, you get 60-80 Mbps. Still usable for streaming and browsing, but you may notice slower file downloads and occasional buffering on 4K.
  • Poor (under 60%): On a 100 Mbps connection, you get under 60 Mbps. This VPN is underperforming. Try switching protocol, server, or reconsider the VPN itself.

On a fast base connection like 500 Mbps or 1 Gbps, even a 20% reduction is fine in absolute terms. The percentage benchmark matters most on slower connections of 50-150 Mbps.

Split Tunneling: Route Only What Needs the VPN

Split tunneling lets you send only specific apps through the VPN tunnel while everything else goes direct. This is the cleanest solution when you need VPN protection for some traffic but want full speed for the rest.

Practical example: you need the VPN for your banking app and work tools, but gaming and Netflix can go direct. Enable split tunneling, add those specific apps to the excluded list, and your gaming ping drops back to baseline while your sensitive traffic stays protected.

Split tunneling is available in NordVPN, ExpressVPN, and Surfshark on most platforms. Mullvad supports it on desktop but not on iOS due to Apple's network extension restrictions.

When Slow Speed Is Not the VPN's Fault

Before blaming the VPN, consider these common causes of slow speeds that have nothing to do with the VPN itself:

  • Your base connection is already slow. A VPN cannot increase your bandwidth. If your ISP gives you 10 Mbps, a VPN gives you at best 8-9 Mbps. Check your baseline first.
  • The server is on another continent. Connecting from Amsterdam to a US server for a European task doubles your routing distance for no reason. Pick a server that makes sense for your use case.
  • Your ISP throttles VPN traffic. Some ISPs slow down connections to known VPN IP ranges or specific ports. Symptoms include fast baseline speeds but consistently poor VPN speeds regardless of server or protocol. Try enabling obfuscation or changing the VPN port in your app settings.

DNS Leak Test: Check While You Are at It

While testing speed, run a DNS leak test at dnsleaktest.com with your VPN active. A DNS leak means your DNS queries (what sites you are looking up) are going through your real ISP's servers instead of through the VPN. This is both a privacy risk and, in some cases, a cause of slower page loading because your ISP's DNS resolver may be slower than your VPN's.

If the test shows your real ISP's DNS servers, your VPN has a DNS leak. Most VPN apps have a DNS leak protection toggle. Enable it. If the leak persists, contact the VPN's support team.

Quick Reference: Speed Test Checklist

  • Test 3 times without VPN, average the results
  • Connect VPN to a nearby server, test 3 times, average
  • Calculate speed retention percentage
  • If under 80%, try switching to WireGuard protocol
  • If still slow, try a different server with lower load
  • Run dnsleaktest.com with VPN active to check for DNS leaks
  • If all servers are slow, contact VPN support or consider switching providers
